abstract.text: Create systems that grow and evolve.

inlineContent.text: The elements that make up an L-system are modules, defined in Lindenmayer as structs that conform to the
protocol.
To create an L-system, you provide it with at least one module as a starting point, called
, and one or more rules.
An L-system maintains its state as a sequence of modules, and when you invoke
, the L-system iterates through all of the modules in it’s state, attempting to invoke one of the rules.
The set of rules within an L-system are evaluated, and the first one matching is used.
If no rules match, then the module is ignored and left in it’s current place.
If a rule matches, then the L-system invokes its
closure, and replaces the module with the set of modules that closure returns.
To create an L-system, define the modules you require and call one of the
initializers on
, such as
, providing it with the module, or modules, that make up the starting set for your L-system.
The following example illustrates defining two modules,
and
, and providing an instance of one to start the L-system:
The working logic of an L-system is captured in its rules.
Use the
function, which returns an new L-system after adding the rule you defined to its list of rules.
When you define a rule, provide it with the type of module that it should match and a closure that produces a list of modules.
The following example adds a rule that matches to the module
, and replaces any instance of
with an instance of
followed by an instance of
A convenient way to define an L-system is chaining the rules one after another.
The full algae example, for example:
When you call
on an L-system, it iterates through its state, applying the rules, and generates a new L-system with the updated state.
You can also call
to repeatedly evolve the L-system.
Read the state of an L-system through its
property, which returns an array of
The instances returned from the array are existential types (meaning they are some type that conforms to
), so expect to interact with them through the properties defined within the
protocol, or by converting the type to an explicit type to access any additional properties your module’s type may include.
Be aware that many L-systems extend the length of the state geometrically as they evolve, so calls to
may be quite expensive and take noticeable, or considerable, time and memory.
Two renderers are included with the Lindenmayer library,
for rendering into a SwiftUI
view, and
for generating a SceneKit
Pass an instance of the L-system to a renderer, which reads through the
and generates a visual representation based on the relevant
that are associated with each Module.
The default implementation for either
or
of
is

The

property, and draws the representation by using the
returned from the property as turtle graphics commands.
The
reads the
property, adding the 3D element and/or updating the state of the renderer, to generate the SceneKit scene.
Lindenmayer comes with a number of built-in modules that you can use, either as a representation of your own modules as a
, or by including them in the set of modules returned by one of your rules.
See
for the set of 2D and 3D built-in modules that the library includes.
In the example above, the modules we created have no representation in either 2D or 3D, so the renderers can’t produce a visual display.
To get a 3D display, extend the modules to provide a 3D rendering command from the property
The two segments are sized and colored differently to make them easy to distinguish for this example.
With the modules, updated, you use the SceneKit renderer to generate a scene:
The resulting scene, rendered in a SceneKit view:
The sister library to Lindenmayer,
, provides SwiftUI views that you can use to quickly display either 2D or 3D representations of the L-systems you create.
The source for this library is open source, and includes a number of example L-systems.

algae_4.alt: A screenshot of a 3D rendering of the algae L-system evolved to display 3 short green segments connected by 2 longer red seegments.

abstract.text: Lindenmayer provides a library to develop and run your own Lindenmayer Systems.
A basic Lindenmayer system.
An L-system that describes a fractal plant, also known as the Barnsley Fern.
An L-system that describes a fractal dragon curve.
An L-system that describes a fractal, or binary, tree.
An L-system that describes a fractal koch curve.
An L-system that describes a fractal sierpinski triangle.
An example of a L-system that produces a tree with monopodial structure.
An example of a L-system that produces a tree with monopodial structure.
An example of a L-system that produces a tree with sympodial structure.
A renderer that generates a 2D graphical representation of an L-system.
A type that provides convenient initializers for L-systems.
Creates a new Lindenmayer system from an initial state.
A type that represents a Lindenmayer system and how it evolves.
A module that represents a state element in an L-system state array and its parameters, if any.
Returns a sequence of render commands to display the content in 2-dimensionals.
Returns a sequence of render commands to display the content in 3-dimensionals.
The set of modules found in sequence while processing the state of an L-system.
A namespace for a collection of built-in modules for use in L-systems.
A parameterized, stochastic Lindenmayer system.
A class that provides reference semantics to access an underlying parameter value type that you provide.
A class that provides probabilistic functions based on the seedable psuedo-random number generator used to create it.
A stochastic Lindenmayer system.
A collection of render commands, both 2D and 3D, built into Lindenmayer.
A value that indicates the state of the drawing should ignore this module.
A renderer that generates a 3D graphical representation of an L-system using SceneKit.
